title: Reverb
summary: Simulates acoustic spaces. Always route via a return track (send), never as an insert on individual elements. High-pass the return at 200Hz — always.
steps: Create a return track: click the '+' button at the bottom of the return section (or Cmd+Option+T), name it 'Reverb'
Drag Reverb from Audio Effects onto the return track
Set Dry/Wet to 100% (the return track is only reverb — the original signal stays on its own track)
Add EQ Eight before the Reverb on the return track: high-pass at 200Hz to remove low-frequency reverb mud
Set Decay (the reverb tail length): Large hall = 3–4s for pads. Small room = 0.3–0.5s for drums/clap. Plate = 1.5s for a medium-sized space
Set Pre-delay to 15ms on pad reverb — this lets the dry attack hit first, then the reverb follows
On each track that should have reverb, turn up its Send knob (A, B, etc.) to feed signal to the reverb return
tips: Never add reverb as an insert effect directly on a track — always use a return send. Insert = each element in its own separate space. Send = everything shares one room
The most common beginner mistake: reverb on sub bass. Sub bass + reverb = muddy mix. Keep the sub clean
More reverb ≠ deeper sound. A too-long decay creates mud. Start at 2.5s and reduce if the mix feels washed out
Solo the reverb return track and listen to just the reverb tail — you should hear it as a clean, high-passed wash. If it sounds muddy, increase the HP frequency

images.caption: Set Dry/Wet to 100% — this must be 100% on a return track (you control wetness from the Send A knob on each source track, not from inside the device). Set Decay Time to 3.5s for pads or 0.4s for drums. Set Pre-delay to 15ms so the dry attack hits before the reverb bloom.

ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
